# Transcrição: 02 Humor 2 - Exaggeration

- **Fonte:** 02 Humor 2 - Exaggeration
- **Duração:** 01:58
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 01:58]

Exaggeration. Exaggeration only works when the audience knows that we're exaggerating. If we're attempting as storytellers to exaggerate in order to fool the audience into believing something is bigger or smaller or greater or lesser than it really was, then exaggeration does not work. That just amounts to a lie. Exaggeration is when we take something and we make it so outlandish that it makes people laugh. So for example, I tell the story of the first Christmas that Alicia and I celebrate. Now, Alicia is Jewish, so she had never had a Christmas tree before, so I allowed her to pick out the Christmas tree. I say in the story that we called it the wild one. It was seven feet wide and four feet tall, and it makes people laugh. Now, the tree is not really seven feet wide and four feet tall. And I don't think any audience member has ever really believed those dimensions. What they believe is that it was a ridiculously shaped tree, but because I exaggerate the dimensions, it makes the audience laugh. In another story, I recently told I confront a man in a parking lot at a rest area. He's driving this enormous truck, and I'm a little nervous about it because I've hit his truck with my minivan. As he steps out of the car, I say he's nine feet tall. No one in the audience believes he's nine feet tall, but when I say nine feet tall, they roar with laughter. I'm telling them he's huge, but when I say he's nine feet tall, that exaggeration increases the chances that they will laugh. So we exaggerate only in instances when the audience understands we're exaggerating, and it's the combination of that understanding plus the truth hidden within the exaggeration that makes people laugh. It's an easy way to add humor to your stories.
